genotype_results.AA: Fast caffeine metabolizer — rapid clearance. Can typically tolerate higher caffeine intake without adverse cardiovascular effects.
genotype_results.AC: Moderate caffeine metabolizer — intermediate clearance speed.
genotype_results.CC: Slow caffeine metabolizer — caffeine stays in system longer. Associated with increased heart attack risk with high coffee consumption (>3 cups/day).

description: Determines how quickly your body breaks down caffeine. Fast metabolizers can drink more coffee without jitteriness; slow metabolizers may experience prolonged effects and should limit intake.

genotype_results.AA: Lactase persistent — can digest dairy into adulthood without issues.
genotype_results.AG: Lactase persistent — can digest dairy normally.
genotype_results.GG: Lactose intolerant — lactase production declines after childhood. Dairy consumption may cause bloating, gas, and digestive discomfort.

description: Controls whether you continue producing the lactase enzyme into adulthood. The A allele arose ~10,000 years ago in European dairy-farming populations.

genotype_results.GG: Normal ALDH2 activity — efficient acetaldehyde metabolism. No flush reaction.
genotype_results.GA: Reduced ALDH2 activity — facial flushing, nausea after drinking alcohol. ~6x increased esophageal cancer risk with regular drinking.
genotype_results.AA: Non-functional ALDH2 — severe alcohol intolerance. Strong flushing, rapid heart rate, nausea. Strongly protective against alcoholism but elevated cancer risk if drinking.

description: The 'Asian flush' gene. Determines how efficiently you break down acetaldehyde, a toxic alcohol metabolite. Common in East Asian populations (~30-50% carry at least one A allele).

genotype_results.CC: Likely perceives cilantro as fresh and herbal — normal cilantro enjoyment.
genotype_results.CA: May have mild soapy perception of cilantro.
genotype_results.AA: Likely perceives cilantro as soapy or unpleasant — genetic basis for cilantro aversion.

description: Variant in an olfactory receptor gene that affects perception of aldehyde chemicals in cilantro leaves. About 14% of people of European ancestry report cilantro tastes like soap.

genotype_results.GG: Non-taster — cannot taste PTC/PROP bitterness. May have higher tolerance for bitter vegetables (broccoli, Brussels sprouts, kale).
genotype_results.GC: Medium taster — moderate perception of bitterness.
genotype_results.CC: Super-taster — strong perception of bitter compounds. May dislike cruciferous vegetables, black coffee, grapefruit, and dark chocolate.

description: Part of the TAS2R38 bitter taste receptor. Super-tasters experience more intense bitter flavors, which can influence dietary choices and nutritional patterns.

genotype_results.GG: Normal CBS activity. Standard B6 and homocysteine metabolism.
genotype_results.GA: Slightly increased CBS activity. May have faster homocysteine clearance.
genotype_results.AA: Increased CBS activity — faster homocysteine to cysteine conversion. May deplete vitamin B6 more rapidly.

description: Cystathionine beta-synthase variant affecting vitamin B6 utilization and homocysteine metabolism pathway.

genotype_results.AA: Normal B12 levels expected.
genotype_results.GG: Non-secretor status — associated with lower vitamin B12 levels. May benefit from B12 supplementation or monitoring.

description: FUT2 secretor status affects gut microbiome composition and vitamin B12 absorption efficiency.

genotype_results.AA: Normal beta-carotene conversion — efficiently converts plant-based carotenoids to vitamin A.
genotype_results.AT: Reduced conversion efficiency (~32% reduction). May benefit from preformed vitamin A sources (eggs, dairy, liver).
genotype_results.TT: Significantly reduced conversion (~69% reduction). Poor converter — plant-based carotenoids are less effective vitamin A sources.

description: Determines how efficiently you convert beta-carotene (carrots, sweet potatoes) into active vitamin A. Poor converters may need animal-sourced vitamin A or supplements.

genotype_results.CC: Normal conversion efficiency.
genotype_results.CT: Modestly reduced beta-carotene conversion.
genotype_results.TT: Reduced conversion — combined with rs12934922 variants may significantly impair vitamin A production from plant sources.

description: Second BCMO1 variant affecting beta-carotene to vitamin A conversion. Effects are additive with rs12934922.

genotype_results.TT: Normal vitamin D binding protein — standard vitamin D levels expected.
genotype_results.GG: Lower vitamin D binding protein levels — associated with ~25% lower serum 25(OH)D. Higher supplementation may be needed.

description: Variant in vitamin D binding protein (GC globulin) affecting circulating vitamin D levels. One of the strongest genetic determinants of vitamin D status.

genotype_results.CC: Normal VDR function — standard vitamin D response.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly altered VDR function.
genotype_results.TT: Altered vitamin D receptor — may need higher vitamin D levels for optimal bone health and immune function.

description: Vitamin D receptor variant affecting calcium absorption and bone mineral density.

genotype_results.AA: Normal methionine synthase reductase — efficient B12 recycling.
genotype_results.GG: Reduced MTRR activity — less efficient B12 utilization. May benefit from higher B12 intake, especially methylcobalamin form.

description: Affects how efficiently your body recycles vitamin B12 in the methionine cycle. Important for methylation and homocysteine control.

genotype_results.CC: Higher average vitamin B6 levels.
genotype_results.CT: Average vitamin B6 levels.
genotype_results.TT: Lower average vitamin B6 levels — may benefit from B6 supplementation.

description: Common variant associated with circulating vitamin B6 (pyridoxal 5'-phosphate) levels.
source: Tanaka et al., Am J Hum Genet 2009

genotype_results.CC: Efficient conversion of plant omega-3 (ALA) to EPA/DHA. Higher arachidonic acid levels.
genotype_results.CT: Intermediate omega-3 conversion efficiency.
genotype_results.TT: Less efficient ALA to EPA/DHA conversion. May benefit more from direct fish oil supplementation rather than plant-based omega-3 sources.

description: Affects desaturase enzyme activity that converts plant-based omega-3 (ALA from flax, chia) into the active EPA/DHA forms found in fish oil.

genotype_results.CC: Lower HDL cholesterol. Benefits most from dietary fat reduction to improve lipid profile.
genotype_results.CT: Intermediate HDL levels.
genotype_results.TT: Higher HDL cholesterol. Less responsive to dietary fat manipulation. HDL levels are more genetically determined.

description: Hepatic lipase variant affecting HDL metabolism and dietary fat response. Influences how much your cholesterol levels change with diet.

genotype_results.CC: Normal glucose sensing in brain and gut.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly altered glucose transport.
genotype_results.TT: Altered glucose transporter — associated with higher habitual sugar consumption (~5-10% more daily sugar intake).

description: GLUT2 glucose transporter variant affecting brain glucose sensing and dietary sugar preferences.
source: Eny et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2008

genotype_results.GG: Higher fat taste sensitivity — can detect low concentrations of fatty acids. May naturally prefer lower-fat foods.
genotype_results.GA: Intermediate fat taste sensitivity.
genotype_results.AA: Reduced fat taste sensitivity — cannot detect fat as easily. May prefer higher-fat foods for satisfaction.

description: Fat taste receptor variant affecting oral fat perception. People with reduced sensitivity may consume more dietary fat to achieve satisfaction.
source: Pepino et al., J Lipid Res 2012

genotype_results.GG: Blue or light-colored eyes (~72% probability of blue eyes).
genotype_results.GA: Green, hazel, or intermediate eye color. Variable expression.
genotype_results.AA: Brown eyes (~95% probability of brown eyes).

description: The single most important genetic variant for eye color. Controls OCA2 expression, which determines melanin production in the iris.

genotype_results.CC: Normal MC1R function — eumelanin production (darker pigmentation).
genotype_results.CT: Carrier — may have reddish hair tones, fair skin, freckling. Mild increase in melanoma risk.
genotype_results.TT: Two MC1R variants — likely red hair, very fair skin, heavy freckling. ~3-4x melanoma risk.

description: Major red hair variant. Shifts melanin production from dark eumelanin to red/yellow pheomelanin. One of several MC1R variants contributing to red hair phenotype.

genotype_results.CC: No effect on hair color from this variant.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly lighter hair color tendency.
genotype_results.TT: Associated with blond hair. Affects hair follicle melanocyte regulation through KITLG enhancer.

description: Regulatory variant affecting KITLG expression specifically in hair follicles. One of several variants contributing to blond hair in Europeans.

genotype_results.AA: Light skin pigmentation — European-type. Reduced melanin in skin.
genotype_results.GG: Darker skin pigmentation — ancestral allele. Higher melanin production.

description: The most significant single variant for light skin pigmentation in European populations. Nearly fixed for the A allele in Europeans, nearly absent in West Africans and East Asians.

genotype_results.GG: Light skin — derived European allele.
genotype_results.CC: Darker skin — ancestral allele.

description: Second major skin pigmentation gene. Works together with SLC24A5 to determine melanin production in skin.

genotype_results.CC: Wet, sticky earwax. Normal apocrine gland secretion and body odor.
genotype_results.CT: Wet earwax type (C is dominant).
genotype_results.TT: Dry, flaky earwax. Reduced body odor — fewer apocrine secretions. Common in East Asian populations (~80-95%).

description: Controls apocrine gland secretion type. The T allele reduces ABCC11 transporter function, leading to dry earwax and less body odor. Also affects axillary (underarm) sweat composition.

genotype_results.CC: Standard hair thickness and sweat gland density.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly thicker hair.
genotype_results.TT: Thicker, straighter hair. Increased sweat gland density. Shovel-shaped incisors. Common in East Asian and Native American populations.

description: EDAR variant affecting ectodermal development. The derived T allele increases hair shaft diameter, affects tooth morphology, and increases eccrine sweat gland density.
source: Fujimoto et al., J Hum Genet 2008

genotype_results.AA: Lower freckling tendency.
genotype_results.GG: Higher freckling tendency, especially with sun exposure.

description: Variant associated with freckling density, particularly in sun-exposed areas.

genotype_results.GG: Normal UV sensitivity — standard sun response.
genotype_results.GA: Slightly increased sun sensitivity.
genotype_results.AA: Increased sun sensitivity — burns more easily. Higher UV damage susceptibility.

description: MC1R variant affecting melanocortin receptor function and UV response. Contributes to sun sensitivity independent of hair/skin color.
source: Bastiaens et al., J Invest Dermatol 2001

genotype_results.GG: Standard eye color (brown if no blue-eye alleles).
genotype_results.GA: May have lighter eye color — increased chance of green or hazel eyes.
genotype_results.AA: Strongly associated with green/hazel eyes and lighter iris pigmentation.

description: OCA2 variant that modifies eye color, particularly contributing to green eye color. Works in combination with HERC2 rs12913832.
source: Duffy et al., Am J Hum Genet 2007

genotype_results.GG: Contributes to lighter eye color.
genotype_results.GA: Intermediate effect.
genotype_results.AA: Contributes to darker eye color.

description: Second OCA2 variant contributing to iris pigmentation variation.

genotype_results.CC: Standard tyrosinase activity — normal melanin production.
genotype_results.CA: Slightly reduced melanin — may have lighter complexion.
genotype_results.AA: Reduced tyrosinase activity — lighter skin and eye color.

description: Tyrosinase is the rate-limiting enzyme in melanin synthesis. This variant affects overall pigmentation levels.

genotype_results.CC: Normal MC1R function.
genotype_results.CG: Carrier — possible reddish tones, increased sun sensitivity.
genotype_results.GG: Strong red hair variant — likely red/auburn hair with fair skin.

description: Another major MC1R red hair variant (D294H). One of the 'R' (strong) MC1R variants.

genotype_results.AA: Contributes to darker hair color.
genotype_results.GG: Associated with lighter/blond hair.

description: TYRP1 variant contributing to eumelanin levels in hair. Part of the polygenic basis of hair color variation.

genotype_results.TT: Lower risk of early-onset baldness.
genotype_results.CC: Increased risk of male pattern baldness.

description: One of several genetic variants contributing to androgenetic alopecia. Hair loss is highly polygenic.

genotype_results.GG: Increased risk of androgenetic alopecia — higher androgen receptor sensitivity.
genotype_results.GA: Moderate baldness risk.
genotype_results.AA: Lower risk of male pattern baldness.

description: Androgen receptor variant on X chromosome. Strongest single genetic predictor of male pattern baldness. Inherited from mother's side.

genotype_results.AA: Standard pigmentation.
genotype_results.GG: Lighter skin pigmentation — variant common in East Asian populations contributing to lighter skin tone.

description: OCA2 variant selected for lighter pigmentation in East Asian populations, through a different mechanism than European skin lightening variants.

genotype_results.AA: Straight hair tendency.
genotype_results.TT: Curly or wavy hair tendency — trichohyalin protein variant affecting hair cross-linking.

description: Variant in trichohyalin, a structural protein in hair follicles. Affects hair fiber shape and curliness in European populations.
source: Medland et al., J Invest Dermatol 2009

genotype_results.CC: Full alpha-actinin-3 expression in fast-twitch fibers — sprint/power advantage. Over-represented in Olympic sprinters.
genotype_results.CT: Intermediate — mix of fast and slow-twitch properties. Versatile athletic profile.
genotype_results.TT: No alpha-actinin-3 (R577X) — endurance advantage. Better muscle efficiency for sustained activity. Over-represented in elite endurance athletes.

description: The 'speed gene'. ACTN3 protein is exclusively expressed in fast-twitch muscle fibers. The X allele (T) causes a beneficial shift toward slow-twitch, oxidative muscle properties.
source: Yang et al., Am J Hum Genet 2003

genotype_results.CC: Normal beta-3 adrenergic receptor — standard fat mobilization during exercise.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly altered fat metabolism efficiency.
genotype_results.TT: Trp64Arg variant — altered fat mobilization. May have less efficient fat burning during endurance exercise. Higher BMI association in some studies.

description: Beta-3 adrenergic receptor variant affecting fat metabolism and thermogenesis. The Arg64 variant may impair lipolysis during exercise.

genotype_results.II: Insertion/Insertion — lower ACE activity. Endurance advantage. Better altitude acclimatization. Over-represented in elite endurance athletes.
genotype_results.ID: Intermediate ACE levels — balanced athletic profile.
genotype_results.DD: Deletion/Deletion — higher ACE activity. Power/strength advantage. Better muscle strength response to training.

description: ACE insertion/deletion polymorphism. The I allele reduces ACE activity, favoring endurance capacity; the D allele increases ACE and favors power/strength development.

genotype_results.GG: Normal PGC-1alpha function — standard mitochondrial biogenesis.
genotype_results.GA: Slightly reduced PGC-1alpha activity.
genotype_results.AA: Gly482Ser variant — reduced mitochondrial biogenesis response to training. May have lower VO2max improvement with endurance training.

description: PGC-1alpha is the master regulator of mitochondrial biogenesis. This variant affects how well your muscles adapt to endurance training.
source: Lucia et al., J Appl Physiol 2005

genotype_results.CC: Normal BDNF secretion — standard mood improvement from exercise.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly reduced BDNF release.
genotype_results.TT: Val66Met — reduced activity-dependent BDNF secretion. May experience less 'runner's high' but exercise is still beneficial.

description: BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic factor) affects neuroplasticity and mood. The Met66 variant reduces activity-dependent BDNF release, potentially affecting exercise-related mood benefits.

genotype_results.GG: Normal collagen type 1 — standard tendon and ligament strength.
genotype_results.TT: Altered collagen — associated with reduced risk of ACL tears and Achilles tendon injuries, but potentially weaker bones.

description: Collagen type I variant affecting connective tissue composition. Paradoxically, the T allele is associated with more flexible tendons (fewer ruptures) but reduced bone mineral density.
source: Posthumus et al., Br J Sports Med 2009

genotype_results.CC: Normal range of motion and tendon stiffness.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly increased flexibility.
genotype_results.TT: Increased flexibility — associated with greater range of motion. Reduced risk of tendinopathy. Common in endurance athletes.

description: Collagen type V variant affecting tendon mechanical properties and flexibility. May influence injury risk and athletic performance.
source: Collins et al., Br J Sports Med 2009

genotype_results.CC: Normal metabolic coupling — standard energy efficiency.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly altered energy metabolism.
genotype_results.TT: Ala55Val — increased mitochondrial uncoupling. May burn more calories at rest (higher metabolic rate) but less energy-efficient for endurance.

description: Uncoupling protein 2 variant affecting metabolic efficiency. Affects how tightly mitochondria couple energy production to ATP synthesis.

genotype_results.TT: Lower risk of Achilles tendinopathy.
genotype_results.AA: Increased risk of Achilles tendon injuries — altered tenascin C in tendon extracellular matrix.

description: Tenascin C variant affecting tendon extracellular matrix composition and injury susceptibility.
source: Mokone et al., Br J Sports Med 2006

genotype_results.GG: Val/Val — 'Warrior': Faster dopamine clearance. Better stress resilience and performance under pressure. Lower baseline cognitive performance in calm settings.
genotype_results.GA: Val/Met — Balanced: Intermediate dopamine metabolism. Good balance of stress resilience and cognitive performance.
genotype_results.AA: Met/Met — 'Worrier': Slower dopamine clearance. Superior working memory, attention, and planning in calm settings. More anxiety-prone under stress.

description: COMT catechol-O-methyltransferase determines dopamine breakdown speed in the prefrontal cortex. Affects stress response, cognitive performance, and pain sensitivity.
source: Stein et al., Am J Med Genet 2006

genotype_results.GG: Higher empathy, better social skills, more optimistic. Greater oxytocin receptor expression — stronger social bonding.
genotype_results.GA: Intermediate empathy and social behavior.
genotype_results.AA: Lower empathy scores on average. May have less social reward sensitivity. Not a deficiency — may be more independent-minded.

description: Oxytocin receptor variant affecting social behavior. GG individuals tend to score higher on empathy measures, handle stress better with social support, and are better at reading emotional cues.

genotype_results.GG: Normal serotonin synthesis — standard emotional regulation.
genotype_results.TT: Higher amygdala reactivity to emotional stimuli — more emotionally responsive. May experience stronger emotional reactions to negative events.

description: Tryptophan hydroxylase 2 variant affecting brain serotonin synthesis. Associated with differences in emotional processing and amygdala activation.

genotype_results.CC: Normal BDNF secretion — standard memory function and neuroplasticity.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly reduced activity-dependent BDNF release. Subtle effects on memory encoding.
genotype_results.TT: Val66Met — reduced BDNF secretion. May have slightly lower episodic memory performance. Higher susceptibility to anxiety. Exercise is particularly beneficial.

description: BDNF Val66Met variant affecting brain-derived neurotrophic factor release. Influences memory formation, learning, and stress resilience.

genotype_results.TT: Short/low-expression allele — higher stress sensitivity. More reactive to environmental stressors. May be more susceptible to depression after adverse life events.
genotype_results.CC: Long/high-expression allele — more resilient to stress. Lower depression risk after adverse events.

description: Functional variant in serotonin transporter gene promoter. Modulates how much serotonin is recycled at synapses, affecting mood regulation and stress vulnerability.

genotype_results.TT: Higher DRD4 expression — potentially increased novelty seeking, curiosity, and exploratory behavior.
genotype_results.CC: Lower DRD4 expression — may be more cautious and risk-averse.

description: Dopamine receptor D4 promoter variant affecting receptor expression. Associated with personality traits related to novelty seeking and exploration.

genotype_results.CC: Standard pitch discrimination ability.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly enhanced pitch discrimination.
genotype_results.TT: Associated with enhanced pitch discrimination ability — not perfect pitch alone, but contributes to musical aptitude.

description: One of several genetic variants associated with perfect pitch (absolute pitch). Musical ability is highly polygenic and requires early training.
source: Theusch et al., Am J Hum Genet 2009

genotype_results.CC: Standard circadian rhythm.
genotype_results.CT: Slight tendency toward evening chronotype.
genotype_results.TT: Evening chronotype preference — natural tendency to stay up later and wake later ('night owl').

description: CLOCK gene variant affecting circadian rhythm. Night owls have different clock gene expression patterns than early birds.

genotype_results.AA: Morning chronotype tendency — natural early riser.
genotype_results.GG: Evening chronotype — 'night owl'. Difficulty waking early. Peak performance later in the day.

description: Classic CLOCK gene variant. The 3111C allele (G) is associated with eveningness, delayed sleep phase, and reduced sleep duration.

genotype_results.CC: Normal adenosine deaminase activity — standard deep sleep patterns.
genotype_results.CT: Reduced ADA activity (~20-30% less). Slower adenosine clearance — increased slow-wave (deep) sleep.
genotype_results.TT: Significantly reduced ADA activity. More deep sleep — higher sleep pressure, more intense slow-wave sleep.

description: Adenosine deaminase variant affecting adenosine metabolism in the brain. Adenosine is the primary sleep pressure molecule — slower clearance means more deep sleep.

genotype_results.CC: Normal sleep duration requirement (~7-9 hours).
genotype_results.CT: May require slightly less sleep than average.
genotype_results.TT: Natural short sleeper — can function well on 4-6 hours of sleep. Very rare variant.

description: The 'short sleeper' gene. Extremely rare variant allowing some people to function normally with significantly less sleep. Affects circadian transcription factor.

genotype_results.CC: Standard sleep duration preference.
genotype_results.GG: Associated with morningness — tend to wake earlier and prefer shorter sleep duration.

description: PER3 (Period 3) is a core circadian clock gene. Variants affect sleep timing, duration, and chronotype preference.
source: Ebisawa et al., Am J Hum Genet 2001

genotype_results.TT: Standard sleep timing.
genotype_results.CC: Morning chronotype — associated with earlier sleep and wake times.

description: Aryl hydrocarbon receptor variant affecting circadian regulation. GWAS-identified chronotype locus.

genotype_results.CC: Normal sleep phase timing.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly delayed sleep onset.
genotype_results.TT: Tendency toward delayed sleep phase — difficulty falling asleep at conventional times.

description: Cryptochrome 1 variant affecting the negative limb of the circadian clock. Associated with delayed sleep phase disorder.

genotype_results.GG: Can smell asparagus metabolites in urine — may notice the distinctive smell after eating asparagus.
genotype_results.GA: Intermediate ability to detect asparagus urine smell.
genotype_results.AA: Cannot smell asparagus metabolites — asparagus anosmia. Unaware of the urine odor change after eating asparagus.

description: Olfactory receptor variant determining whether you can detect the sulfurous compounds in urine after eating asparagus. About 40% of people cannot smell them.

genotype_results.CC: Normal — no reflex sneezing from bright light.
genotype_results.CT: May experience photic sneeze reflex.
genotype_results.TT: Photic sneeze reflex — sneezing when exposed to bright light (ACHOO syndrome). Affects ~18-35% of the population.

description: Associated with autosomal dominant compulsive helio-ophthalmic outbursts of sneezing (ACHOO syndrome). The reflex is triggered by sudden exposure to bright light.

genotype_results.AA: Contributes to blue eye color when combined with other light-eye alleles.
genotype_results.GG: Contributes to darker (non-blue) eye color.

description: Additional OCA2 variant contributing to eye color determination.

genotype_results.CC: Normal insulin secretion and glucose metabolism.
genotype_results.CT: Modestly impaired insulin secretion — may benefit from moderate carb restriction.
genotype_results.TT: Impaired insulin secretion — higher carbohydrate sensitivity. Low-glycemic diet particularly beneficial. ~2x T2D risk.

description: Strongest common type 2 diabetes risk variant. Affects insulin secretion in response to carbohydrate intake. Dietary carbohydrate restriction is especially beneficial for risk allele carriers.

genotype_results.TT: Normal appetite regulation and satiety signals.
genotype_results.TA: Slightly increased appetite — ~1.5 kg higher average body weight.
genotype_results.AA: Altered satiety signaling — increased appetite, ~3 kg higher average body weight. Physical activity fully attenuates this genetic effect.

description: The 'obesity gene' variant affects appetite regulation through FTO's role in ghrelin mRNA modification. Physical activity completely counteracts the genetic risk.

genotype_results.GG: Normal dopamine receptor density — standard reward processing.
genotype_results.GA: Modestly reduced D2 receptor density — slightly increased reward-seeking.
genotype_results.AA: Reduced D2 receptor density (~30-40%) — higher reward-seeking behavior. May be more susceptible to addictive behaviors including food, substances, and gambling.

description: Variant near DRD2 dopamine receptor affecting receptor density in the brain's reward circuitry. Influences response to pleasurable stimuli and addiction vulnerability.
source: Noble et al., Arch Gen Psychiatry 1998

genotype_results.GG: Normal amygdala reactivity — standard anxiety response.
genotype_results.TT: Higher amygdala activation — may be more prone to anxiety, especially in stressful environments. Not diagnostic — many factors contribute to anxiety.

description: Tryptophan hydroxylase 2 variant in serotonin synthesis pathway. Affects brain serotonin production and emotional processing in the amygdala.

genotype_results.CC: Lower serotonin 2A receptor expression.
genotype_results.CT: Intermediate receptor levels.
genotype_results.TT: Higher 5-HT2A receptor expression — associated with stronger placebo responses and more vivid dreams.

description: Serotonin 2A receptor variant. Higher expression is associated with stronger placebo effects in clinical trials and more vivid REM sleep dreaming.
source: Leuchter et al., Am J Psychiatry 2009

genotype_results.AA: Standard calcium channel function.
genotype_results.GG: Associated with enhanced verbal fluency and creative thinking in some studies. Also associated with slightly elevated mood disorder risk.

description: Calcium channel variant affecting neuronal signaling. Tentatively linked to creative thinking ability, but also associated with bipolar disorder susceptibility.

genotype_results.CC: Normal alpha-2A adrenergic receptor function.
genotype_results.GG: Altered prefrontal cortex norepinephrine signaling — may have difficulty with sustained attention tasks.

description: Alpha-2A adrenergic receptor variant affecting prefrontal cortex function. Influences attention regulation and response to ADHD medications like guanfacine.

genotype_results.GG: Val/Val — faster dopamine clearance, lower pain sensitivity. Better tolerance of acute pain. May need lower opioid doses.
genotype_results.GA: Val/Met — intermediate pain sensitivity.
genotype_results.AA: Met/Met — slower dopamine clearance, higher pain sensitivity. May experience more intense pain and require higher analgesic doses.

description: COMT Val158Met affects not only stress response but also pain perception. Met/Met individuals have higher pain sensitivity through effects on enkephalin and endorphin signaling.

genotype_results.CC: Normal sweet taste preference.
genotype_results.AA: Higher preference for sweet foods.

description: Variant associated with sweet food preference and consumption patterns.

genotype_results.CC: Low perception of grapefruit bitterness — likely enjoys grapefruit.
genotype_results.CT: Moderate bitterness perception.
genotype_results.TT: Strong grapefruit bitterness perception — may dislike grapefruit and similar citrus.

description: Bitter taste receptor variant specifically affecting perception of naringin, the bitter compound in grapefruit.

genotype_results.CC: Fast alcohol metabolism — rapid alcohol to acetaldehyde conversion. Protective against alcohol dependence but causes unpleasant flushing.
genotype_results.CT: Intermediate — faster than average alcohol metabolism.
genotype_results.TT: Normal/slow alcohol metabolism — standard alcohol processing speed.

description: Alcohol dehydrogenase variant. The fast allele (C) is common in East Asian populations and is strongly protective against alcoholism due to unpleasant acetaldehyde accumulation.
source: Li et al., Am J Hum Genet 2011

genotype_results.TT: Standard tolerance to circadian disruption.
genotype_results.TA: Intermediate tolerance to shift work and jet lag.
genotype_results.AA: May have better tolerance of circadian disruption — less affected by jet lag and shift work.

description: Aurora kinase A variant affecting cell cycle progression and circadian clock interaction.

genotype_results.TT: Normal adiponectin levels — standard metabolic profile.
genotype_results.GG: Lower adiponectin levels — associated with insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome. Weight management particularly important.

description: Adiponectin gene variant affecting this anti-inflammatory hormone's levels. Lower adiponectin is associated with obesity, insulin resistance, and cardiovascular risk.
source: Menzaghi et al., J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2007

genotype_results.TT: Normal MTHFR activity at this position.
genotype_results.TG: Mildly reduced MTHFR activity at A1298C locus.
genotype_results.GG: Reduced MTHFR activity — compound heterozygosity with C677T significantly impairs folate metabolism.

description: Second MTHFR variant affecting folate metabolism. Primarily significant when combined with C677T variant (compound heterozygote).
source: van der Put et al., Am J Hum Genet 1998

genotype_results.AA: Secretor — susceptible to norovirus infection.
genotype_results.AG: Secretor — susceptible to most norovirus strains.
genotype_results.GG: Non-secretor — significantly resistant to many norovirus strains. ~20% of Europeans are non-secretors.

description: FUT2 non-secretors lack ABH blood group antigens in saliva and mucosal surfaces. This makes them resistant to many norovirus strains that use these antigens for cell entry.

genotype_results.GG: Associated with blue eye color.
genotype_results.GA: Intermediate effect on eye color.
genotype_results.AA: Associated with brown eye color.

description: OCA2 regulatory variant contributing to eye color determination.
source: Duffy et al., Am J Hum Genet 2007

genotype_results.TT: Normal nitric oxide production — standard vasodilation capacity.
genotype_results.CC: Reduced NOS3 expression — lower nitric oxide production. May have less exercise-induced vasodilation and higher blood pressure tendency.

description: Endothelial nitric oxide synthase variant affecting blood vessel dilation. Influences blood pressure, exercise capacity, and cardiovascular health.

genotype_results.AA: Normal myostatin function — standard muscle growth regulation.
genotype_results.AG: Slightly reduced myostatin activity — may have easier time building muscle mass.
genotype_results.GG: Reduced myostatin — enhanced muscle growth potential. Myostatin is a negative regulator of muscle growth.

description: Myostatin variant affecting muscle growth regulation. Myostatin limits muscle growth — reduced function variants allow greater muscle hypertrophy response to training.
source: Santiago et al., Med Sci Sports Exerc 2011

genotype_results.AA: Standard neuregulin signaling.
genotype_results.GG: Associated with leadership role occupancy in twin studies. May have traits that predispose to taking charge in group settings.

description: Neuregulin 1 variant tentatively associated with leadership behavior. Effect is very small and heavily influenced by environment and experience.
source: De Neve et al., Leadership Quarterly 2013

genotype_results.GG: Higher IL-6 response to exercise — stronger inflammatory response. May need longer recovery between intense sessions.
genotype_results.CC: Lower IL-6 response to exercise — may recover faster from intense training. Less exercise-induced inflammation.

description: IL-6 variant affecting inflammatory response to exercise. Influences recovery time and muscle damage markers after intense physical activity.
source: Yamin et al., J Appl Physiol 2008

genotype_results.GG: Normal TNF-alpha response to exercise.
genotype_results.GA: Increased TNF-alpha production after exercise. May experience more post-exercise muscle soreness.
genotype_results.AA: High TNF-alpha response — prolonged inflammation after intense exercise. May benefit from anti-inflammatory nutrition strategies.

description: TNF-alpha variant affecting inflammatory response magnitude. High producers may experience more delayed-onset muscle soreness and longer recovery times.
source: Yamin et al., J Appl Physiol 2008

genotype_results.AA: Lactase persistent — can absorb calcium from dairy products efficiently throughout life.
genotype_results.AG: Lactase persistent — dairy calcium absorption maintained.
genotype_results.GG: Lactose intolerant — may have reduced calcium absorption from dairy. Consider non-dairy calcium sources or supplements.

description: Lactase persistence directly affects ability to use dairy as a calcium source. Lactose-intolerant individuals may need alternative calcium strategies for bone health.

genotype_results.CC: Lower tendency for unibrow (synophrys).
genotype_results.CT: Intermediate eyebrow pattern.
genotype_results.TT: Higher tendency for connected eyebrows (unibrow).

description: HLA region variant associated with eyebrow thickness and connectivity. One of several genes affecting facial hair patterns.

genotype_results.CC: Standard memory performance.
genotype_results.CT: Enhanced episodic memory — T allele carriers perform better on memory recall tasks.
genotype_results.TT: Enhanced episodic memory performance — better recall of personal events and word lists in cognitive tests.

description: KIBRA protein interacts with key memory molecules in the hippocampus. T allele carriers consistently show better episodic memory performance across multiple studies.

genotype_results.GG: Better tanning ability — more melanin production response to UV.
genotype_results.GA: Moderate tanning ability.
genotype_results.AA: Reduced tanning — tendency to burn rather than tan with sun exposure.

description: OCA2 variant affecting melanin production response to ultraviolet radiation.

genotype_results.GG: Standard coffee consumption patterns.
genotype_results.GA: May tend toward slightly lower coffee consumption.
genotype_results.AA: Associated with lower habitual coffee consumption — may be more sensitive to coffee's inflammatory effects.

description: TNF-alpha variant influencing habitual coffee consumption through interaction with inflammatory pathways.

genotype_results.GG: Normal bone mineral density — standard collagen type I structure.
genotype_results.TT: Lower bone mineral density — increased osteoporosis risk. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation recommended.

description: Collagen type I variant affecting bone matrix composition and density. The T allele is associated with reduced bone strength.
source: Mann et al., J Clin Invest 2001

genotype_results.CC: Higher tendency toward weight gain — altered insulin signaling pathway regulation.
genotype_results.GG: Standard weight regulation.

description: INSIG2 variant affecting insulin-induced gene 2, which regulates lipid metabolism. CC genotype is associated with higher BMI.

genotype_results.AA: Higher paraoxonase activity — better organophosphate pesticide detoxification.
genotype_results.GG: Lower paraoxonase activity — slower pesticide detoxification. May benefit more from organic produce.

description: Paraoxonase 1 variant affecting ability to metabolize organophosphate pesticides and oxidized lipids. GG individuals may benefit more from choosing organic foods.
source: Costa et al., Annu Rev Med 2003

genotype_results.CC: Lactase persistent — can digest lactose into adulthood.
genotype_results.CT: Lactase persistent (C is dominant).
genotype_results.TT: Lactose intolerant — reduced lactase production after childhood.

description: Alternative marker for lactase persistence, in linkage disequilibrium with rs4988235.

genotype_results.GG: Normal Factor V — standard clotting regulation.
genotype_results.GA: Factor V Leiden carrier — 3-8x increased clotting risk. Important for travel, surgery, and contraceptive decisions.
genotype_results.AA: Homozygous Factor V Leiden — 50-80x increased clotting risk. May require prophylactic anticoagulation.

description: Factor V Leiden — the most common inherited clotting disorder. Important for understanding personal thrombosis risk, especially before surgery, long flights, or starting hormonal contraception.

genotype_results.CC: Higher 5-HT2A expression — may have slightly higher impulsivity.
genotype_results.CT: Intermediate serotonin 2A receptor levels.
genotype_results.TT: Lower 5-HT2A expression — may be less impulsive.

description: Serotonin 2A receptor promoter variant affecting receptor expression. Influences impulsivity, decision-making, and response to serotonergic drugs.
source: Jakubczyk et al., J Psychiatr Res 2012

genotype_results.CC: Normal complement C3 function.
genotype_results.CG: Fast C3 form — more active complement system. May have stronger innate immune response.
genotype_results.GG: C3 fast variant — enhanced complement activation. Stronger initial immune response but may increase inflammatory risk.

description: Complement component 3 variant. The 'fast' form has higher hemolytic activity. Affects innate immune defense strength and inflammation.

genotype_results.wt/wt: Normal CCR5 receptor — standard HIV susceptibility.
genotype_results.wt/\u039432: One copy of delta-32 deletion — partial HIV resistance. Slower disease progression if infected.
genotype_results.\u039432/\u039432: Homozygous CCR5-delta32 — highly resistant to HIV-1 infection (R5-tropic strains). ~1% of Northern Europeans.

description: 32-base pair deletion in CCR5 co-receptor for HIV. Homozygotes are essentially immune to most HIV strains. The 'Berlin patient' was cured of HIV via transplant from a delta-32 donor.

genotype_results.TT: Standard FOXO3 activity.
genotype_results.GG: Associated with increased longevity — FOXO3 G allele is over-represented in centenarians across multiple populations.

description: FOXO3 is one of the most consistently replicated longevity genes across populations. The G allele enhances cellular stress resistance and DNA repair.

genotype_results.GG: Higher CETP activity — standard HDL levels.
genotype_results.GA: Intermediate CETP activity.
genotype_results.AA: Reduced CETP activity — higher HDL cholesterol. Associated with longevity and cardiovascular protection.

description: CETP transfers cholesterol between lipoproteins. Reduced CETP activity raises HDL ('good') cholesterol. This variant has been associated with exceptional longevity in centenarian studies.

genotype_results.CC: Standard VDR start codon — normal vitamin D receptor function.
genotype_results.CT: Intermediate vitamin D receptor activity.
genotype_results.TT: Longer VDR protein — less active vitamin D receptor. May need higher vitamin D levels for optimal immune and bone function.

description: VDR FokI variant affecting vitamin D receptor protein length and activity. Influences bone mineral density, immune function, and cancer risk.

genotype_results.TT: GC1s — higher vitamin D binding protein levels. Good vitamin D transport but may need more to achieve optimal free levels.
genotype_results.GG: GC2 — lower vitamin D binding protein. Lower total vitamin D but relatively more free (bioavailable) vitamin D.

description: Vitamin D binding protein variant affecting total and free vitamin D levels. Determines the ratio of bound to free (active) vitamin D in circulation.
source: Powe et al., N Engl J Med 2013

genotype_results.CC: Non-taster — can eat cruciferous vegetables without strong bitterness. Likely enjoys broccoli, kale, Brussels sprouts.
genotype_results.CT: Medium taster — moderate perception of vegetable bitterness.
genotype_results.TT: Super-taster for glucosinolate bitterness — may avoid healthy cruciferous vegetables due to intense bitter taste.

description: Second TAS2R38 variant affecting bitter taste perception. Super-tasters may eat fewer vegetables, potentially missing cancer-protective phytochemicals.

genotype_results.CC: No HLA-DQ2.5 — very low risk of celiac disease.
genotype_results.CT: Heterozygous HLA-DQ2.5 carrier — increased celiac disease susceptibility. ~3% lifetime risk.
genotype_results.TT: Homozygous HLA-DQ2.5 — highest genetic risk for celiac disease. ~10% lifetime risk.

description: HLA-DQ2.5 determines genetic susceptibility to celiac disease (gluten intolerance). Necessary but not sufficient — most carriers never develop celiac disease.

genotype_results.AA: Higher GSTP1 activity — more efficient detoxification of environmental toxins.
genotype_results.GG: Lower GSTP1 activity — reduced ability to detoxify polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, pesticides, and heavy metals.

description: Glutathione S-transferase Pi variant affecting phase II detoxification. Determines how efficiently you clear environmental chemicals and carcinogens.
source: Ali-Osman et al., J Biol Chem 1997

genotype_results.CC: APOE ε4 haplotype (if rs429358=C) or ε3 — dietary fat significantly affects cholesterol levels.
genotype_results.CT: May carry ε2 allele — check combined APOE genotype.
genotype_results.TT: APOE ε2 carrier — more responsive to dietary cholesterol changes. Higher triglyceride risk with high-carb diets.

description: APOE genotype affects how your body handles dietary fats and cholesterol. ε4 carriers are most sensitive to saturated fat; ε2 carriers are more affected by carbohydrates.
source: Minihane et al., Proc Nutr Soc 2007

genotype_results.CC: Normal PPAR-alpha function — standard fatty acid oxidation.
genotype_results.GG: Leu162Val — altered lipid metabolism. May have different response to high-fat diets and fibrate medications.

description: PPAR-alpha is a nuclear receptor controlling fat metabolism genes. This variant affects fatty acid oxidation efficiency and response to dietary fats.
source: Tai et al., J Clin Invest 2002

genotype_results.GG: Normal MTHFR activity — standard folate needs (400mcg/day DFE).
genotype_results.GA: Mildly reduced MTHFR (~65% activity) — may benefit from slightly higher folate intake.
genotype_results.AA: Significantly reduced MTHFR (~30% activity) — higher folate needs. Consider methylfolate supplementation (600-1000mcg). Especially important before and during pregnancy.

description: The most clinically significant nutrigenomics variant. Affects conversion of folic acid to active methylfolate. TT genotype needs active folate forms.

genotype_results.II: Lower ACE activity — better endurance performance. Greater blood pressure reduction with regular exercise.
genotype_results.ID: Intermediate ACE levels — balanced fitness response.
genotype_results.DD: Higher ACE activity — better strength/power gains from training. Less blood pressure reduction benefit from aerobic exercise.

description: ACE I/D polymorphism is one of the most studied fitness genetics markers. Influences both athletic potential and cardiovascular response to training.

genotype_results.GG: Normal PPAR-alpha — good fat oxidation response to endurance training.
genotype_results.CC: Reduced fat oxidation adaptation to endurance training. May need more training volume for same improvements.

description: PPAR-alpha variant affecting metabolic adaptation to endurance training. Influences fat utilization during prolonged exercise.
source: Ahmetov et al., J Strength Cond Res 2006

genotype_results.CC: Glu27 — higher fat loss response to exercise. Better lipolysis during physical activity.
genotype_results.GG: Gln27 — reduced exercise-induced lipolysis. May need higher exercise intensity for same fat-loss benefits.

description: Beta-2 adrenergic receptor variant affecting exercise-induced fat mobilization. Glu27Gln polymorphism influences how effectively exercise triggers fat burning.

genotype_results.AA: Normal deiodinase 1 — standard T4 to T3 conversion.
genotype_results.GG: Reduced T4 to T3 conversion — may have relatively lower T3 levels. Can affect metabolism and energy.

description: Deiodinase 1 variant affecting conversion of inactive thyroid hormone (T4) to active form (T3). May influence metabolic rate and energy levels.
source: Panicker et al., J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2008

genotype_results.CC: Normal deiodinase 2 — efficient brain T3 production.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly reduced brain T3 conversion.
genotype_results.TT: Thr92Ala — reduced brain T3 levels. May affect mood and cognition. Some patients feel better on combination T4/T3 therapy.

description: Deiodinase 2 variant primarily affecting T3 levels in the brain. The Ala92 variant may contribute to hypothyroid symptoms despite normal blood TSH.
source: Appelhof et al., J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2005

genotype_results.CC: Normal zinc transporter — standard insulin secretion.
genotype_results.CT: Intermediate zinc transport in beta cells.
genotype_results.TT: Reduced ZnT8 function — modestly increased type 2 diabetes risk. Adequate zinc intake may be particularly important.

description: Zinc transporter 8 variant in pancreatic beta cells. Zinc is essential for insulin crystal formation and storage. This variant affects insulin secretion efficiency.

genotype_results.CC: Normal HIF-1alpha — standard altitude acclimatization.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly enhanced hypoxic response.
genotype_results.TT: Enhanced HIF response — may acclimatize to altitude faster. Better performance at high elevation.

description: Hypoxia-inducible factor 1-alpha variant affecting the body's response to low oxygen. Important for altitude athletes and high-elevation performance.

genotype_results.GG: Normal prohormone convertase — standard appetite regulation.
genotype_results.GA: Slightly altered prohormone processing.
genotype_results.AA: Impaired prohormone processing — associated with increased obesity risk through effects on appetite hormones.

description: Prohormone convertase 1 variant affecting processing of appetite-regulating hormones including proinsulin, proglucagon, and POMC.

genotype_results.GG: Normal leptin production — standard satiety signaling.
genotype_results.GA: Slightly lower leptin levels.
genotype_results.AA: Lower leptin levels — may have reduced satiety signaling. Could contribute to overeating tendency.

description: Leptin gene promoter variant affecting leptin hormone production. Leptin signals satiety to the brain — lower levels may reduce feeling of fullness after meals.
source: Mammès et al., Hum Mol Genet 1998

genotype_results.AA: Normal leptin receptor — standard satiety response.
genotype_results.GG: Reduced leptin receptor function — may be less sensitive to satiety signals. Higher tendency toward weight gain.

description: Leptin receptor variant affecting how well your brain responds to leptin satiety signals. Reduced sensitivity can lead to overeating.

genotype_results.TT: Normal MC4R — standard appetite regulation.
genotype_results.TC: Slightly increased appetite and meal size.
genotype_results.CC: Increased appetite — ~1.5 kg higher body weight per risk allele. Tendency toward larger meal portions.

description: Melanocortin 4 receptor variant affecting appetite regulation in the hypothalamus. MC4R is a critical node in the leptin-melanocortin appetite control pathway.

genotype_results.GG: Val/Val — faster COMT activity. More efficient estrogen detoxification. Lower breast cancer risk from estrogen exposure.
genotype_results.GA: Val/Met — intermediate estrogen metabolism.
genotype_results.AA: Met/Met — slower COMT activity. Less efficient estrogen breakdown. May have slightly higher estrogen-related cancer risk.

description: COMT also metabolizes catechol estrogens. Slower metabolism (Met/Met) may lead to higher levels of potentially genotoxic estrogen metabolites.

genotype_results.CC: Arg/Arg — associated with slightly higher birth weight due to enhanced beta-1 adrenergic signaling.
genotype_results.CG: Arg/Gly — intermediate effect on birth weight.
genotype_results.GG: Gly/Gly — associated with lower birth weight. Reduced adrenergic receptor activity.

description: Beta-1 adrenergic receptor variant influencing fetal growth. Birth weight is highly polygenic — this SNP contributes a small effect alongside FTO (rs9939609) and GCK (rs1799884). Higher birth weight is associated with the C allele.

genotype_results.GG: More likely to have a smooth chin without a cleft.
genotype_results.GA: Intermediate likelihood of cleft chin — partial dimple possible.
genotype_results.AA: More likely to have a cleft (dimpled) chin — a visible vertical indentation in the chin.

description: Cleft chin is a Y-shaped fissure on the chin caused by incomplete fusion of the jawbone during development. While traditionally described as a dominant trait, GWAS studies show it is polygenic with contributions from rs11684042 and rs7556462.

genotype_results.TT: Less likely to have cheek dimples.
genotype_results.TC: May have subtle cheek dimples — partial expression.
genotype_results.CC: More likely to have cheek dimples — caused by variation in the zygomaticus major muscle inserting into the skin.

description: Cheek dimples result from a split in the zygomaticus major facial muscle. MYH9 encodes a myosin heavy chain involved in muscle structure. Genetic associations are preliminary and dimple inheritance is not fully understood.

genotype_results.CC: Less likely to be able to voluntarily wiggle ears.
genotype_results.CT: Intermediate — may be able to learn ear wiggling with practice.
genotype_results.TT: More likely to have the ability to voluntarily wiggle ears — related to vestigial auricular muscle control.

description: The ability to wiggle one's ears is a vestigial trait controlled by the auricular muscles. This locus near KALRN (Kalirin) has been associated with voluntary ear movement in GWAS. About 10-20% of people can wiggle their ears.

genotype_results.GG: More likely to have attached (joined) earlobes — lobe connects directly to the side of the head.
genotype_results.GA: Intermediate earlobe attachment — partially free-hanging lobes.
genotype_results.AA: More likely to have detached (free-hanging) earlobes — lobe hangs freely below the point of attachment.

description: Earlobe attachment is a classic genetics teaching example, though it is actually polygenic rather than simple Mendelian. Variants near EDAR and rs2045434 contribute to whether earlobes hang free or attach directly to the head.
source: Shaffer et al., American Journal of Human Genetics 2017

genotype_results.GG: Associated with shorter eyelashes.
genotype_results.GA: Intermediate eyelash length.
genotype_results.AA: Associated with longer eyelashes — FOXC2 region influences hair follicle development in the eyelid.

description: Eyelash length varies widely and is influenced by multiple genes. The FOXC2 region (rs12916399, rs11855019) plays a role in hair follicle morphology. Longer lashes are generally a recessive-leaning polygenic trait.

genotype_results.CC: Thinner facial hair — less dense beard growth in males.
genotype_results.CT: Intermediate facial hair thickness.
genotype_results.TT: Thicker facial hair — denser, coarser beard growth in males. LNX1 influences androgen-mediated hair follicle activity.

description: Beard thickness and density are influenced by androgen sensitivity and hair follicle gene expression. LNX1 (rs756853) and rs12565727 have been associated with facial hair variation in large GWAS. Primarily relevant in males; effect is androgen-dependent.

genotype_results.CC: Higher 2D:4D ratio — index finger relatively longer compared to ring finger. Associated with lower prenatal androgen exposure.
genotype_results.AA: Lower 2D:4D ratio — ring finger relatively longer than index finger. Associated with higher prenatal androgen exposure.

description: The ratio of index finger (2D) to ring finger (4D) length is a sexually dimorphic trait influenced by prenatal testosterone exposure. LIN28B and SMOC1 (rs2267675) contribute to digit ratio variation. Males typically have lower 2D:4D ratios than females.
source: Medland et al., Journal of Human Genetics 2010

genotype_results.GG: More likely to have prominent iris patterns — crypts (diamond-shaped openings) and Wolfflin nodules. Lighter iris pigmentation reveals structural patterns more clearly.
genotype_results.GA: Intermediate iris pattern visibility — moderate crypt and furrow structures.
genotype_results.AA: Fewer visible iris patterns — darker, more uniformly pigmented irises tend to obscure structural features like crypts and furrows.

description: Iris patterns (crypts, furrows, rings, and pigment dots) are distinct from eye color. HERC2 (rs12913832) and OCA2 (rs1800407) affect melanin density in the iris stroma, which determines how visible structural patterns are. Lighter irises show more pattern detail.

genotype_results.CC: More likely to develop all four wisdom teeth (third molars).
genotype_results.CT: Intermediate — may develop 2-3 wisdom teeth or have partial eruption.
genotype_results.TT: More likely to have congenitally missing wisdom teeth — one or more third molars may never develop. PAX9 is critical for molar tooth initiation.

description: About 20-25% of people are congenitally missing at least one wisdom tooth. PAX9 is a transcription factor essential for tooth development, and variants in this region influence whether third molars form. This is an evolutionary trend — jaw size reduction in modern humans leaves less room for wisdom teeth.
source: Nieminen et al., Human Molecular Genetics 2003

genotype_results.CC: Stable blood sugar regulation — less likely to experience mood changes when hungry.
genotype_results.CT: Moderate blood sugar fluctuation — may occasionally feel irritable when meals are delayed.
genotype_results.TT: Greater blood sugar variability — more prone to 'hanger' (hunger-driven irritability). Impaired insulin secretion can lead to sharper glucose dips between meals.

description: TCF7L2 affects insulin secretion and glucose homeostasis. Carriers of the T allele experience greater blood sugar swings, which can trigger irritability, difficulty concentrating, and mood changes when meals are skipped or delayed.
source: Grant et al., Nature Genetics 2006; Gonder-Frederick et al., J Behav Med 1997

genotype_results.GG: Normal glucokinase activity — stable fasting glucose and hunger signaling.
genotype_results.GA: Slightly elevated fasting glucose — mild changes in hunger-mood coupling.
genotype_results.AA: Reduced glucokinase promoter activity — higher fasting glucose variability. May experience stronger hunger-driven mood shifts and irritability.

description: GCK encodes glucokinase, the glucose sensor in pancreatic beta cells. Promoter variants affect the threshold for insulin release, influencing how rapidly blood sugar drops trigger hunger signals and associated mood changes.
source: Weedon et al., Diabetes 2006; Rose et al., Diabetologia 2009

genotype_results.AA: Normal carbonic anhydrase 6 activity — standard taste bud function and food acceptance.
genotype_results.AG: Mildly altered taste sensitivity — may be somewhat more selective about food textures and flavors.
genotype_results.GG: Reduced CA6 activity — altered saliva composition affects taste perception. Associated with heightened sensitivity to food textures and increased food selectivity.

description: CA6 (carbonic anhydrase 6) is secreted into saliva and influences taste bud development and maintenance. Variants can alter taste perception breadth, contributing to food selectivity and 'picky eating' behavior, especially in childhood.
source: Padiglia et al., Chem Senses 2010; Barbarossa et al., PLoS One 2015

genotype_results.GG: Non-taster — lower bitter sensitivity. Generally more accepting of diverse foods including bitter vegetables.
genotype_results.GC: Medium taster — moderate bitter perception. Mildly selective about bitter or strongly-flavored foods.
genotype_results.CC: Super-taster — heightened bitter perception across many foods. Strong predictor of food selectivity, avoidance of cruciferous vegetables, and 'picky eating' in children and adults.

description: TAS2R38 bitter taste receptor sensitivity is one of the strongest genetic predictors of food selectivity. Super-tasters perceive bitter compounds intensely, leading to avoidance of many vegetables, herbs, and fermented foods.
source: Kim et al., Science 2003; Mennella et al., BMC Genetics 2010

genotype_results.CC: Normal TRPV1 sensitivity — standard capsaicin response. Typical burning sensation from spicy foods.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly reduced capsaicin receptor sensitivity — may tolerate spicy foods better than average.
genotype_results.TT: Reduced TRPV1 sensitivity — diminished response to capsaicin. Higher natural tolerance for spicy foods with less burning and pain.

description: TRPV1 is the capsaicin receptor — the ion channel that detects heat and the 'burn' of chili peppers. The rs8065080 variant (Val585Ile) reduces receptor sensitivity, meaning carriers feel less pain from spicy food.
source: Cantero-Recasens et al., Mol Biol Evol 2005; Lv et al., PLoS One 2017

genotype_results.GG: Normal TRPV1 channel function — standard spice/heat sensitivity.
genotype_results.GC: Mildly altered capsaicin sensitivity — subtle differences in spice tolerance.
genotype_results.CC: Altered TRPV1 function — modified heat and capsaicin perception. May contribute to higher spice tolerance alongside other TRPV1 variants.

description: A second variant in the TRPV1 capsaicin receptor gene (Met315Ile) that modifies ion channel gating properties. Combined with rs8065080, it helps explain individual variation in spicy food tolerance and preference.
source: Kim et al., J Dent Res 2004; Lv et al., PLoS One 2017

genotype_results.CC: Normal TAS1R2 sweet receptor function — standard sweet taste sensitivity.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly altered sweet receptor — may have modified sweet taste threshold, subtly shifting preference toward savory.
genotype_results.TT: Reduced sweet receptor sensitivity — higher sugar threshold needed to perceive sweetness. Associated with preference for savory over sweet foods.

description: TAS1R2 encodes one subunit of the sweet taste receptor heterodimer. Variants affect how intensely sweetness is perceived: those with reduced sensitivity tend to prefer savory foods or require more sugar to satisfy a sweet craving.
source: Fushan et al., Curr Biol 2009; Dias et al., Appetite 2015

genotype_results.TT: Normal TAS1R3 function — typical sweet taste perception.
genotype_results.TC: Mildly altered sweet receptor complex — subtle differences in sweetness intensity perception.
genotype_results.CC: Altered TAS1R3 subunit — modified sweet receptor sensitivity. May shift food preference away from sweet toward umami or savory flavors.

description: TAS1R3 encodes the second subunit of the sweet taste receptor (and also the umami receptor). Variants here affect both sweet and umami perception, influencing whether a person gravitates toward sweet or savory foods.
source: Reed et al., Chem Senses 2006; Fushan et al., Curr Biol 2009

genotype_results.CC: Normal TAS1R1 function — standard umami taste perception. Typical response to glutamate-rich foods (parmesan, tomatoes, mushrooms, soy sauce).
genotype_results.CA: Mildly reduced umami sensitivity — may perceive savory/meaty flavors as slightly less intense.
genotype_results.AA: Reduced umami receptor function — diminished ability to taste glutamate. May use more MSG, soy sauce, or seasoning to achieve satisfying savory flavor.

description: TAS1R1 encodes a subunit of the umami (savory) taste receptor. Variants reduce sensitivity to L-glutamate, the amino acid responsible for the 'fifth taste' found in aged cheeses, fermented foods, meat broths, and tomatoes.
source: Shigemura et al., J Biol Chem 2009; Chen et al., PNAS 2009

genotype_results.TT: Normal umami receptor complex — standard glutamate perception.
genotype_results.TC: Mildly altered TAS1R3 — subtle changes in umami intensity perception.
genotype_results.CC: Altered TAS1R3 function — modifies both umami and sweet receptor complexes. May experience reduced umami taste, affecting preference for fermented and savory-rich foods.

description: TAS1R3 is shared between the sweet and umami receptor complexes. This variant modifies the umami receptor heterodimer (TAS1R1+TAS1R3), affecting perception of glutamate and nucleotide flavors in food.
source: Reed et al., Chem Senses 2006; Raliou et al., Chem Senses 2009

genotype_results.GG: Normal vitamin C transporter function — efficient intestinal absorption and tissue distribution of ascorbic acid.
genotype_results.GA: Mildly reduced SLC23A1 activity — slightly lower circulating vitamin C levels. Standard dietary intake usually sufficient.
genotype_results.AA: Reduced vitamin C transporter function — lower plasma vitamin C levels even with adequate dietary intake. May benefit from increased citrus, berries, and peppers or supplementation.

description: SLC23A1 encodes the sodium-dependent vitamin C transporter (SVCT1), responsible for intestinal absorption of ascorbic acid. Variants reduce transport efficiency, leading to lower circulating vitamin C independent of dietary intake.
source: Timpson et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2010; Duell et al., Cancer Epidemiol 2013

genotype_results.CC: Normal SLC23A1 expression — standard vitamin C absorption capacity.
genotype_results.CT: Mildly reduced transporter expression — slightly lower average plasma vitamin C.
genotype_results.TT: Reduced SLC23A1 expression — lower vitamin C absorption efficiency. Combined with rs33972313 variants, may significantly reduce circulating ascorbic acid levels.

description: A second variant in SLC23A1 affecting vitamin C transporter expression levels. Acts additively with rs33972313 to influence overall vitamin C status and may modify scurvy risk in populations with marginal dietary intake.
source: Timpson et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2010; Michels et al., Nutr Rev 2013

genotype_results.CC: Normal APOA5 function — efficient triglyceride-rich lipoprotein metabolism and vitamin E transport.
genotype_results.CG: Mildly altered lipid metabolism — slightly lower vitamin E carried in lipoproteins.
genotype_results.GG: Reduced APOA5 activity — higher triglycerides and altered lipoprotein composition. May lead to lower bioavailable vitamin E despite adequate dietary intake.

description: APOA5 regulates triglyceride-rich lipoproteins that carry fat-soluble vitamins including vitamin E (alpha-tocopherol). Variants that raise triglycerides alter vitamin E distribution and bioavailability in tissues.
source: Major et al., J Nutr 2011; Ferrucci et al., PLoS Med 2009

genotype_results.CC: Normal triglyceride metabolism — standard vitamin E transport via lipoproteins.
genotype_results.CG: Mildly elevated triglycerides — may slightly reduce vitamin E bioavailability.
genotype_results.GG: Elevated triglycerides — altered lipoprotein particle composition reduces vitamin E delivery to tissues. May benefit from vitamin E-rich diet (nuts, seeds, olive oil) or supplementation.

description: rs964184 near ZPR1/APOA5 is a well-replicated triglyceride-associated locus. Higher triglycerides alter the lipoprotein particles that transport vitamin E, potentially reducing tissue delivery of alpha-tocopherol.
source: Teslovich et al., Nature 2010; Major et al., J Nutr 2011

genotype_results.CC: Full alpha-actinin-3 in fast-twitch fibers — strong genetic foundation for power-based sports. Sprinters and strength athletes are over-represented with this genotype.
genotype_results.CT: Mix of fast-twitch and slow-twitch properties — versatile athletic profile suited to a wide range of sports.
genotype_results.TT: No alpha-actinin-3 — shifted toward endurance-type muscle fibers. May excel in distance running, cycling, or swimming rather than power events.

description: A broad indicator of athletic predisposition based on muscle fiber composition. The ACTN3 gene is sometimes called the 'gene for speed' because it shapes whether your muscles favor explosive power or sustained endurance.
source: Yang et al., Am J Hum Genet 2003

genotype_results.II: Lower ACE activity — favors endurance. Over-represented in elite distance athletes and mountaineers.
genotype_results.ID: Balanced ACE activity — intermediate athletic profile with potential in both power and endurance.
genotype_results.DD: Higher ACE activity — associated with greater strength and power output. More common in elite sprinters and power athletes.

description: The ACE insertion/deletion variant influences cardiovascular efficiency and muscle performance. Together with ACTN3, it is one of the most studied genes in sports genetics.

genotype_results.CC: Optimal fast-twitch fiber activation — tends to gain strength quickly with resistance training. Greater power output gains per training cycle.
genotype_results.CT: Moderate strength training response — steady gains with consistent training.
genotype_results.TT: Slower initial strength gains but potentially better endurance adaptations. May need higher training volume for hypertrophy.

description: How your muscles respond to strength training. People with more fast-twitch fibers tend to see quicker strength gains, while those with more slow-twitch fibers may respond better to higher-rep, endurance-style training.
source: Clarkson et al., Med Sci Sports Exerc 2005

genotype_results.CC: Normal adrenergic receptor function — standard metabolic support for strength training adaptation.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly altered energy mobilization during resistance exercise — may affect recovery between sets.
genotype_results.TT: Reduced adrenergic signaling — may experience slower metabolic adaptation to heavy training. Extra recovery time between sessions could help.

description: The ADRB3 receptor helps mobilize stored energy during intense exercise. Variants can affect how efficiently your body fuels strength training sessions and recovers between workouts.
source: Szendrei et al., Br J Sports Med 2016

genotype_results.CC: Normal BDNF secretion — efficient neural signaling supports strong hand-eye coordination and motor skill learning.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly reduced activity-dependent BDNF release — may need more practice repetitions to lock in fine motor skills.
genotype_results.TT: Lower BDNF secretion — motor learning may take longer, but consistent practice can overcome this. Hand-eye coordination tasks may require more deliberate training.

description: BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic factor) strengthens neural connections involved in motor coordination. Higher BDNF activity supports faster learning of tasks that require precise hand-eye timing, like catching, hitting, or throwing.

genotype_results.GG: Normal nitric oxide production — efficient vasodilation supports quick heart rate recovery after exercise.
genotype_results.GT: Slightly reduced NO production — heart rate may take a bit longer to return to baseline after intense effort.
genotype_results.TT: Lower nitric oxide synthesis — slower post-exercise heart rate recovery. May benefit from longer cool-down periods.

description: How quickly your heart rate drops back to resting after exercise is partly determined by nitric oxide signaling. Faster recovery generally indicates better cardiovascular fitness and autonomic nervous system function.

genotype_results.GG: Arg16 homozygous — enhanced beta-2 receptor function supports faster heart rate recovery and bronchodilation during exercise.
genotype_results.GA: Intermediate receptor sensitivity — standard recovery profile.
genotype_results.AA: Gly16 homozygous — reduced receptor density with sustained stimulation. Heart rate may stay elevated slightly longer post-exercise.

description: Beta-2 adrenergic receptors regulate heart rate and airway dilation during exercise. This variant affects how quickly these receptors respond to calm the cardiovascular system after exertion.
source: Snyder et al., J Appl Physiol 2006

genotype_results.GG: Enhanced thermoregulatory response — better peripheral vasodilation in heat. May maintain performance better in hot conditions.
genotype_results.GA: Intermediate heat response — standard thermoregulation under heat stress.
genotype_results.AA: Reduced adrenergic-mediated vasodilation — may struggle more in high-heat conditions. Hydration and acclimatization strategies are especially important.

description: Your ability to exercise in the heat depends partly on how well blood vessels near the skin dilate to release body heat. Beta-2 receptor variants influence this thermoregulatory response.
source: Heled et al., J Appl Physiol 2013

genotype_results.GG: Normal nitric oxide production — efficient oxygen delivery and vasodilation supports longer breath-hold times.
genotype_results.GT: Slightly reduced NO synthesis — moderate breath-hold capacity.
genotype_results.TT: Lower nitric oxide levels — reduced oxygen efficiency may shorten comfortable breath-hold duration. Breathing exercises can help improve this.

description: How long you can comfortably hold your breath is influenced by how efficiently your body uses available oxygen. Nitric oxide helps blood vessels dilate and improves oxygen delivery to tissues, extending the time before you feel the urge to breathe.
source: Modig et al., Undersea Hyperb Med 2017

genotype_results.CC: Normal metabolic rate regulation — standard oxygen consumption at rest supports typical breath-hold duration.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly altered resting metabolic rate — minimal impact on breath-holding.
genotype_results.TT: Altered metabolic signaling — may have different baseline oxygen consumption patterns. Breath-hold training can still significantly improve duration.

description: The ADRB3 receptor influences resting metabolic rate and oxygen consumption. Lower baseline oxygen demand can mean more time before carbon dioxide buildup triggers the breathing reflex.

genotype_results.CC: Full fast-twitch fiber activation — strong explosive power for vertical jumping. Elite jumpers are significantly over-represented with this genotype.
genotype_results.CT: Mixed fiber profile — good jumping ability with proper training.
genotype_results.TT: Fewer fast-twitch fibers — vertical jump may be lower on average, but plyometric training can still produce meaningful improvements.

description: Jumping height is one of the most heritable athletic traits. Fast-twitch muscle fibers generate the explosive force needed for powerful vertical leaps, and ACTN3 is a key determinant of fast-twitch fiber function.
source: Eynon et al., Eur J Appl Physiol 2009

genotype_results.CC: Normal collagen V structure — typical tendon stiffness supports efficient energy transfer during jumps.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly altered tendon compliance — may affect elastic energy storage in tendons.
genotype_results.TT: More compliant tendons — could reduce explosive force transfer. Plyometric training to improve tendon stiffness may be especially beneficial.

description: Tendon stiffness matters for jumping because stiff tendons store and release elastic energy more efficiently. The COL5A1 gene affects collagen structure in tendons and ligaments, influencing how much spring you get from each jump.
source: Posthumus et al., Br J Sports Med 2011

genotype_results.TT: Normal collagen V expression — standard tendon resilience and fatigue resistance during prolonged exercise.
genotype_results.TC: Slightly altered collagen structure — moderate fatigue resistance.
genotype_results.CC: Altered collagen V levels — tendons may fatigue more quickly under repetitive stress. Proper warm-up and pacing strategies become more important.

description: Muscle fatigue during exercise is not just about the muscles themselves — tendons and connective tissue play a major role. COL5A1 variants affect the structural integrity of connective tissue under repeated stress.
source: Collins et al., Am J Sports Med 2010

genotype_results.CC: Normal cartilage intermediate layer protein — standard joint and connective tissue durability during prolonged activity.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly increased CILP levels — may experience earlier onset of exercise-related stiffness.
genotype_results.TT: Higher CILP expression — associated with increased susceptibility to disc and cartilage fatigue. Recovery between sessions may need extra attention.

description: CILP is found in cartilage and connective tissue and can inhibit tissue repair signals. Higher levels may mean your joints and connective tissue tire out more quickly during sustained exercise.

genotype_results.CC: Robust BDNF signaling — faster motor sequence learning. Picking up and remembering choreography comes more naturally.
genotype_results.CT: Moderate BDNF activity — can learn choreography with regular practice, may need a few extra run-throughs.
genotype_results.TT: Reduced activity-dependent BDNF release — may need more rehearsal to memorize movement sequences. Breaking routines into smaller chunks can help.

description: Learning choreography requires your brain to encode complex movement sequences. BDNF strengthens the neural pathways involved in motor learning, making it easier to pick up and retain dance routines and coordinated movement patterns.

genotype_results.GG: Val/Val — faster dopamine clearance. Quick cognitive processing but may find it harder to sustain focus during long rehearsals.
genotype_results.GA: Val/Met — balanced dopamine levels. Good blend of cognitive flexibility and sustained attention for learning routines.
genotype_results.AA: Met/Met — slower dopamine clearance. Excellent sustained focus and working memory for memorizing complex sequences, but may feel performance anxiety.

description: COMT controls how fast dopamine is broken down in the prefrontal cortex. This affects working memory and motor planning — both crucial for learning and performing choreographed movement sequences under pressure.
source: Noohi et al., Behav Brain Res 2014

genotype_results.CC: Fast-twitch dominant — quick direction changes, powerful accelerations. Natural advantage in sports requiring agility like basketball, soccer, and tennis.
genotype_results.CT: Balanced muscle fiber profile — good overall agility with proper training.
genotype_results.TT: Endurance-oriented fibers — may need to work harder on change-of-direction speed but can build excellent movement efficiency.

description: Physical agility combines speed, coordination, and the ability to change direction quickly. Fast-twitch muscle fibers provide the rapid force production needed for sharp cuts, pivots, and reactive movements.
source: Eynon et al., J Sci Med Sport 2010

genotype_results.CC: Normal BDNF — strong neural plasticity supports rapid motor adaptation and agile movement patterns.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly reduced BDNF secretion — agility can still be developed with focused training drills.
genotype_results.TT: Lower BDNF activity — reactive agility may develop more slowly. Agility ladder and sport-specific drills can help compensate.

description: Agility is not just about muscles — your brain needs to rapidly process visual cues and coordinate movement responses. BDNF supports the neural plasticity required for quick, adaptive movements.
source: Helm et al., J Int Soc Sports Nutr 2017

genotype_results.CC: Normal BDNF levels — strong neuroplasticity supports good proprioception and balance control.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly reduced BDNF — balance is largely trainable, so moderate impact on overall stability.
genotype_results.TT: Lower BDNF secretion — may have slightly less precise proprioceptive feedback. Balance training (yoga, single-leg exercises) is especially beneficial.

description: Balance requires constant communication between your vestibular system, muscles, and brain. BDNF helps maintain and strengthen these neural pathways, affecting how quickly you adapt to unstable surfaces and maintain postural control.

genotype_results.CC: Normal D2 receptor density — standard dopaminergic motor control supports good balance.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly reduced D2 receptor density — minimal impact on balance under normal conditions.
genotype_results.TT: Reduced D2 receptor density — may have slightly less precise motor control. Balance and proprioception exercises can help maintain stability.

description: Dopamine D2 receptors in the basal ganglia help fine-tune motor control and postural adjustments. Lower receptor density can subtly affect the precision of balance corrections, especially during complex movements.

genotype_results.CC: Full alpha-actinin-3 — maximum fast-twitch fiber force production. Associated with higher baseline strength and greater potential for strength development.
genotype_results.CT: Intermediate strength potential — can still develop impressive strength with consistent training.
genotype_results.TT: No alpha-actinin-3 — lower initial strength ceiling but better suited to endurance tasks. Strength can still be built with progressive overload.

description: Raw physical strength depends heavily on fast-twitch muscle fiber composition. ACTN3 is the most replicated gene associated with muscle strength across dozens of studies in different populations.
