source: Erskine et al., Eur J Appl Physiol 2014

genotype_results.DD: Higher ACE activity — associated with greater muscle strength and power. More common among elite strength athletes.
genotype_results.ID: Intermediate ACE levels — balanced strength potential.
genotype_results.II: Lower ACE activity — favors endurance over raw strength. May build strength more gradually.

description: The ACE gene influences muscle growth signaling and blood flow regulation. The D allele promotes greater muscle hypertrophy and power output, complementing ACTN3 as a key strength determinant.
source: Folland et al., Eur J Appl Physiol 2000

genotype_results.GG: Val/Val — faster dopamine clearance in prefrontal cortex. Quick cognitive processing and faster reaction times under pressure.
genotype_results.GA: Val/Met — balanced dopamine metabolism. Good reaction time with moderate stress resilience.
genotype_results.AA: Met/Met — slower dopamine breakdown. Excellent focused attention but reaction times may be slightly slower under high-pressure situations.

description: Reaction time depends on how quickly your brain processes a stimulus and initiates a motor response. COMT influences dopamine levels in the prefrontal cortex, which affects the speed of cognitive processing and decision-making in split-second situations.

genotype_results.CC: Normal BDNF — efficient neural transmission supports fast sensory-motor coupling and quick reflexes.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly reduced BDNF — minor impact on reflex speed. Regular practice of reactive drills can maintain sharp reflexes.
genotype_results.TT: Lower BDNF activity — neural signal speed may be slightly reduced. Reaction-time training drills can help sharpen reflexes.

description: BDNF supports the myelination and strength of neural connections between sensory and motor neurons. Faster neural transmission means quicker reflexes when you need to react to a ball, opponent, or unexpected obstacle.

genotype_results.CC: Full fast-twitch fiber activation — built for speed. This genotype is found in nearly every studied Olympic sprinter.
genotype_results.CT: Mixed fiber type — can develop good speed with sprint-specific training.
genotype_results.TT: Endurance-oriented fibers — top-end sprint speed may be limited, but can still be a fast recreational runner.

description: Sprint speed is one of the most genetically influenced athletic traits. ACTN3 determines whether your fast-twitch fibers produce the explosive force needed for maximal sprinting speed.
source: Yang et al., Am J Hum Genet 2003

genotype_results.CC: Normal iron regulation — standard oxygen transport to working muscles during sprints.
genotype_results.CG: Slightly increased iron absorption — may support higher hemoglobin levels and better oxygen delivery during intense efforts.
genotype_results.GG: Higher iron absorption (H63D variant) — potentially enhanced oxygen carrying capacity. Monitor iron levels as excess can be harmful.

description: Sprinting relies on rapid oxygen delivery to muscles. The HFE gene regulates iron absorption, and iron is essential for hemoglobin production. Slightly increased iron levels can improve oxygen transport during short, intense efforts.

genotype_results.CC: Strong BDNF signaling — picks up new motor skills and techniques faster. Practice sessions are more productive for skill acquisition.
genotype_results.CT: Moderate BDNF activity — good technique learning with consistent practice.
genotype_results.TT: Reduced BDNF release — technical skills may take longer to develop. Benefits from more frequent, shorter practice sessions with focused repetition.

description: Learning sports technique is fundamentally a motor learning task. BDNF drives the strengthening of neural pathways each time you practice a movement, making it a key factor in how quickly you master new skills.

genotype_results.GG: Val/Val — quick cognitive processing supports rapid tactical adaptation. May learn game-sense aspects of technique faster.
genotype_results.GA: Val/Met — balanced profile. Good at both learning new techniques and executing under pressure.
genotype_results.AA: Met/Met — superior working memory and attention to detail. Excels at mastering precise, technical movements but may overthink under competition pressure.

description: Sports technique combines motor learning with cognitive processing. COMT affects dopamine in areas of the brain responsible for motor planning, working memory, and attention — all critical for mastering complex athletic skills.
source: Noohi et al., Behav Brain Res 2014

genotype_results.CC: Sprint-optimized fast-twitch fibers — built for explosive, short-distance speed. Found in virtually 100% of elite male sprinters studied.
genotype_results.CT: Mixed fiber profile — capable sprinter with proper training. Can compete well at sub-elite levels.
genotype_results.TT: Endurance-shifted fibers — sprinting is not your strongest suit genetically, but short-distance speed can still improve significantly with training.

description: Sprinting ability specifically — not just general speed, but your capacity for all-out effort over short distances. This is one of the most strongly genetic athletic traits, with ACTN3 being the primary determinant of fast-twitch fiber function.

genotype_results.CC: Strong BDNF activity — enhanced motor learning and rhythm coordination. Picking up new dance moves tends to come naturally.
genotype_results.CT: Moderate BDNF levels — can develop good dance skills with practice. May need a few extra repetitions to lock in new moves.
genotype_results.TT: Lower BDNF secretion — dance skill acquisition may be slower, but dedicated practice absolutely overcomes this. Many talented dancers have this genotype.

description: Dancing combines rhythm perception, motor coordination, spatial awareness, and emotional expression. BDNF plays a role in the neural plasticity that lets you translate music into coordinated body movements.

genotype_results.GG: Val/Val — quick cognitive processing. Adapts easily to tempo changes and improvisation on the dance floor.
genotype_results.GA: Val/Met — balanced dopamine metabolism. Good combination of creativity and precision for dance.
genotype_results.AA: Met/Met — higher dopamine in prefrontal cortex. May have stronger emotional expression in dance and better memory for complex routines.

description: Dancing is as much a cognitive activity as a physical one. COMT influences the dopamine levels that affect creativity, emotional expression, and motor sequence memory — all essential elements of dance performance.

genotype_results.CC: Normal FAAH activity — standard endocannabinoid breakdown. May experience a typical runner's high after sustained exercise.
genotype_results.CA: Reduced FAAH activity — slower anandamide breakdown. Likely to experience a more noticeable runner's high during moderate-to-vigorous exercise.
genotype_results.AA: Significantly reduced FAAH activity — anandamide levels stay elevated longer. Tends to experience an intense, prolonged runner's high. May find it easier to get 'hooked' on exercise.

description: The runner's high is driven by your endocannabinoid system, not endorphins as was long believed. FAAH breaks down anandamide, a natural cannabinoid your body produces during exercise. Less FAAH activity means more anandamide sticks around, creating a stronger euphoric feeling.
source: Raichlen et al., J Exp Biol 2012

genotype_results.GG: Normal endothelial nitric oxide synthase — efficient vasodilation and oxygen delivery to working muscles during aerobic exercise.
genotype_results.GT: Slightly reduced NO production — moderate impact on peak oxygen uptake.
genotype_results.TT: Lower nitric oxide synthesis — reduced vasodilation capacity may limit peak VO2 max. Consistent aerobic training can partially compensate.

description: VO2 max — the maximum rate your body can use oxygen during exercise — is the gold standard measure of aerobic fitness. Nitric oxide from NOS3 helps blood vessels dilate, improving blood flow and oxygen delivery to muscles during intense exercise.

genotype_results.CC: Normal mitochondrial coupling — standard energy production efficiency during aerobic exercise.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly altered mitochondrial uncoupling — may affect the efficiency of ATP production during sustained effort.
genotype_results.TT: Increased mitochondrial uncoupling — less efficient ATP production per oxygen molecule consumed, but potentially better thermoregulation during exercise.

description: UCP2 affects how efficiently your mitochondria convert oxygen into usable energy (ATP). This uncoupling protein creates a trade-off: more efficient energy production versus better heat dissipation during exercise.
source: Buemann et al., Int J Obes 2001

genotype_results.GA: Carrier of the minor allele — may have altered inflammatory response to heat stress. Take extra precautions in hot environments during exercise.
genotype_results.GG: Normal LZTFL1 function — standard heat tolerance and inflammatory response during hot-weather exercise.
genotype_results.AA: Two copies of the minor allele — potentially heightened inflammatory response to heat stress. Heat acclimatization protocols are especially important.

description: LZTFL1 gained attention for its role in immune and inflammatory responses. Variants in this gene may influence how your body handles the combined stress of heat and physical exertion through inflammatory pathways.

genotype_results.GG: Val/Val — Higher competitive drive. Faster dopamine clearance keeps you cool under pressure, which can fuel a love of head-to-head challenges.
genotype_results.GA: Val/Met — Moderate competitive streak. You enjoy a good contest but don't need to win everything to feel satisfied.
genotype_results.AA: Met/Met — Less driven by direct competition. You're more likely to compete with yourself than with others — personal bests over leaderboards.

description: COMT Val158Met affects dopamine levels in the prefrontal cortex, influencing how rewarding you find competitive situations. Note: environment, upbringing, and culture play a much larger role in competitiveness than any single gene.

genotype_results.GG: Normal D2 receptor density — standard reward processing from competitive wins.
genotype_results.GA: Slightly reduced D2 receptors — may seek out more intense competitive thrills to get the same dopamine hit.
genotype_results.AA: A1/A1 — Reduced D2 receptor density. Winning may feel extra rewarding, potentially driving stronger competitive behavior.

description: The Taq1A variant near DRD2 affects dopamine receptor availability, influencing how much reward you experience from winning. Environmental factors like sports culture and family values are far more important in shaping competitiveness.
source: Comings & Blum, Progress in Brain Research 2000

genotype_results.CC: Val/Val — Normal BDNF secretion supports resilient neural circuits. You may bounce back more easily from setbacks.
genotype_results.CT: Val/Met — Slightly reduced activity-dependent BDNF. You might need a bit more mental effort to push through tough stretches, but you absolutely can.
genotype_results.TT: Met/Met — Reduced BDNF release during stress. Setbacks may hit harder initially, but many people with this genotype develop exceptional determination through practice.

description: BDNF Val66Met influences neuroplasticity and stress resilience, which can affect how easily you bounce back from difficulty. Your actual determination is shaped far more by life experiences, habits, and mindset than by this variant.

genotype_results.GG: Val/Val — Faster dopamine clearance. You may power through challenges by staying level-headed under pressure.
genotype_results.GA: Val/Met — Balanced dopamine metabolism. A nice blend of steady persistence and flexible thinking.
genotype_results.AA: Met/Met — Higher baseline dopamine. You may think deeply before persisting on a task, sometimes overthinking — but when committed, you dig in.

description: COMT affects prefrontal dopamine tone, which can influence how you sustain effort under difficulty. Real determination comes from motivation, values, and habits — genetics plays only a small supporting role.

genotype_results.AA: More likely to lean toward extroversion — you probably recharge through social interaction.
genotype_results.AG: Somewhere in the ambivert range — you enjoy both social time and solo time.
genotype_results.GG: More likely to lean introverted — you may prefer smaller gatherings and need quiet time to recharge.

description: A GWAS-identified locus near WSCD2 associated with sociability and extraversion. Introversion and extroversion exist on a spectrum, and your environment, culture, and life experiences shape where you land far more than genetics.

genotype_results.CC: Slightly higher tendency toward sociability — parties might be your thing.
genotype_results.CT: Balanced social energy — you can work a room or enjoy a quiet evening with equal ease.
genotype_results.TT: Slightly lower social drive — you might prefer a good book over a big gathering, and that's perfectly fine.

description: GWAS-identified variant associated with extraversion traits. Social energy patterns are influenced by dozens of genes working together, each contributing only a tiny effect. Your personality is overwhelmingly shaped by experience and choice.

genotype_results.GG: Higher oxytocin receptor expression — associated with a sunnier outlook and greater social support-seeking, both of which can boost optimism.
genotype_results.GA: Intermediate oxytocin receptor function. Your glass might be half full or half empty depending on the day.
genotype_results.AA: Lower oxytocin receptor expression — you may have a more cautious or realistic outlook. Many would call this practical rather than pessimistic.

description: The oxytocin receptor gene influences social bonding and emotional processing, which can color your general outlook. But optimism is largely a skill that can be practiced — genetics just nudges the starting point.

genotype_results.GG: Standard oxytocin receptor function — typical range of emotional resilience.
genotype_results.GA: Slightly altered OXTR expression — may process positive and negative emotions a bit differently.
genotype_results.AA: Associated with higher sensitivity to social environment — in supportive settings this can actually enhance positive outlook.

description: A second OXTR variant that influences how you process emotional experiences. In positive environments, carriers of the A allele can actually be more optimistic than average — it's about sensitivity to context, not a fixed trait.
source: Lucht et al., Progress in Neuropsychopharmacology & Biological Psychiatry 2009

genotype_results.GG: Normal D2 receptor density — standard reward reinforcement for sustained effort.
genotype_results.GA: Slightly reduced D2 receptors — you might need bigger milestones to keep going, but once hooked on a goal, you stick with it.
genotype_results.AA: A1/A1 — Lower D2 density. You may find it harder to stay motivated by small rewards but can develop exceptional persistence when the goal truly matters to you.

description: DRD2/ANKK1 Taq1A affects how your brain processes the small dopamine rewards that keep you going during long tasks. But true persistence is built through habits and purpose — genetics is just one small ingredient.

genotype_results.GG: Val/Val — Quick dopamine turnover. You may shift focus more easily between tasks rather than grinding on one thing.
genotype_results.GA: Val/Met — Balanced persistence. You can focus deeply when needed but also know when to pivot.
genotype_results.AA: Met/Met — Higher sustained dopamine. You may find it easier to stay locked in on a task for extended periods.

description: COMT influences dopamine availability in the prefrontal cortex, which relates to sustained attention and focus. Actual persistence depends heavily on passion, training, and environment.

genotype_results.CC: Slightly higher genetic propensity toward pet ownership — you might be a natural animal person.
genotype_results.CT: Average tendency — whether you have a pet probably depends more on your living situation than your DNA.
genotype_results.TT: Slightly lower genetic propensity — doesn't mean you don't love animals, just that the urge to bring one home might be less hardwired.

description: GWAS studies of over 35,000 twin pairs identified genetic loci associated with pet ownership. Yes, really — there's a small genetic component to whether you end up with a furry companion. But housing, lifestyle, and allergies matter way more.

genotype_results.TT: Slightly higher affinity for animal companionship — your genome may nudge you toward having a pet.
genotype_results.TC: Average range — pet ownership is likely driven by your life circumstances.
genotype_results.CC: Slightly lower genetic inclination toward pet ownership — you might be more of a plant parent.

description: A second GWAS-identified locus associated with pet ownership in large population studies. The genetic effect is tiny — your childhood experiences with animals, available space, and lifestyle are the real deciding factors.

genotype_results.CC: Normal adenosine receptor function — standard dream recall frequency.
genotype_results.CT: Altered adenosine signaling — you may wake slightly more during REM sleep, giving your brain more chances to encode dreams into memory.
genotype_results.TT: Higher adenosine sensitivity — more frequent sleep-wake transitions during the night may mean you remember your dreams more often.

description: ADORA2A variants affect adenosine receptor sensitivity, influencing sleep architecture and the transitions between sleep stages. More brief awakenings during REM sleep = more dream memories. Keeping a dream journal has a much bigger effect than genetics.
source: Rétey et al., Human Molecular Genetics 2007

genotype_results.CC: Standard adenosine 2A receptor activity — typical dream recall patterns.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly altered sleep microarchitecture — may experience more vivid dream recall.
genotype_results.TT: Modified adenosine signaling — associated with differences in sleep depth and REM transitions that may affect how often you remember dreams.

description: The ADORA2A gene plays a key role in sleep pressure and architecture. This variant affects how your brain transitions between sleep stages, which can influence whether dreams get consolidated into waking memory. Stress, sleep quality, and interest in dreams matter much more.

genotype_results.GG: Higher oxytocin receptor expression — associated with greater self-esteem, social confidence, and feeling secure in relationships.
genotype_results.GA: Intermediate oxytocin receptor function — moderate baseline self-assurance.
genotype_results.AA: Lower OXTR expression — you may be more self-critical or cautious in social settings, but this also drives careful preparation and self-awareness.

description: Oxytocin influences feelings of trust and social security, which contribute to self-confidence. But confidence is largely built through experience, accomplishments, and supportive relationships — your genes just set a subtle starting point.

genotype_results.CC: Val/Val — Robust BDNF signaling supports confident learning from experience. Positive feedback loops build self-assurance more easily.
genotype_results.CT: Val/Met — Slightly reduced activity-dependent BDNF. You might need a few more wins to build lasting confidence, but it absolutely happens.
genotype_results.TT: Met/Met — Lower BDNF activity may mean confidence takes longer to build. On the flip side, you may be more thoughtful and measured in your self-assessment.

description: BDNF supports neuroplasticity — the brain's ability to rewire from positive experiences. This can influence how quickly self-confidence builds. But practice, mentoring, and personal growth are the real confidence builders.

genotype_results.GG: Val/Val — Quick dopamine clearance in the prefrontal cortex. You might find it easier to act decisively and resist temptation.
genotype_results.GA: Val/Met — Balanced dopamine metabolism. Decent willpower with a healthy ability to also be flexible.
genotype_results.AA: Met/Met — Higher prefrontal dopamine. You may deliberate more before acting — which can look like indecision, or like very thoughtful self-regulation.

description: COMT influences dopamine levels in the prefrontal cortex, the brain region responsible for executive function and self-control. But self-discipline is overwhelmingly a skill built through habit formation, environment design, and practice.

genotype_results.GG: Normal D2 density — standard ability to delay gratification.
genotype_results.GA: Slightly reduced D2 receptors — immediate rewards may be a bit more tempting, but awareness of this tendency is half the battle.
genotype_results.AA: A1/A1 — Lower D2 receptor availability. You might find delayed gratification trickier, but structured routines and accountability systems can absolutely compensate.

description: Dopamine D2 receptor availability influences how your brain weighs immediate vs. future rewards, a core component of self-discipline. Environment and habit design are far more powerful tools for building discipline than raw genetics.

genotype_results.AA: Morning chronotype tendency — may feel an afternoon energy dip that makes napping appealing.
genotype_results.AG: Intermediate circadian pattern — whether you nap probably depends more on your schedule than your genes.
genotype_results.GG: Evening chronotype — you may be less inclined to nap because your energy naturally peaks later in the day.

description: The CLOCK gene influences your circadian rhythm, which affects when you feel alert and when you feel sleepy. Morning types who wake early often experience a stronger afternoon dip. But napping habits are mostly shaped by culture, schedule, and lifestyle.

genotype_results.CC: Normal adenosine receptor sensitivity — standard daytime sleepiness patterns.
genotype_results.CT: Altered adenosine signaling — you may build up sleep pressure faster during the day, making naps more tempting.
genotype_results.TT: Higher adenosine sensitivity — you likely feel daytime sleepiness more intensely. A short power nap might be your secret weapon.

description: ADORA2A affects how sensitive you are to adenosine, the molecule that builds up during waking hours and makes you sleepy. Higher sensitivity means stronger 'nap signals' in the afternoon. But caffeine, activity level, and sleep quality matter much more.
source: Rétey et al., Human Molecular Genetics 2007

genotype_results.GG: Val/Val — Quick dopamine clearance. You may be more comfortable with unfamiliar situations because stress doesn't build up as easily.
genotype_results.GA: Val/Met — Balanced approach to novelty. You enjoy trying new things but also appreciate your comfort zone.
genotype_results.AA: Met/Met — Higher dopamine levels may make novel situations feel more intense. You might prefer to research before jumping in — which means when you do try something new, you're really into it.

description: COMT influences how your prefrontal cortex handles the uncertainty that comes with new experiences. But openness to experience is powerfully shaped by culture, education, and personal history — genes explain at most 5-10% of the variation.

genotype_results.GG: Normal D2 density — new experiences provide a standard reward signal.
genotype_results.GA: Slightly reduced D2 receptors — you may crave more novelty because each new experience provides a bigger relative dopamine boost.
genotype_results.AA: A1/A1 — Lower receptor density may drive stronger novelty-seeking as your brain looks for larger reward signals. The upside: you're probably a great travel companion.

description: Dopamine D2 receptor availability shapes how rewarding new experiences feel. Lower D2 density can drive more novelty-seeking as the brain pursues bigger reward signals. But your actual openness to new things is mostly about upbringing, values, and opportunity.
source: Comings & Blum, Progress in Brain Research 2000

genotype_results.GG: Normal D2 density — standard reward from spectator experiences.
genotype_results.GA: Slightly reduced D2 receptors — the emotional highs and lows of watching sports may feel more intense, making you a more passionate fan.
genotype_results.AA: A1/A1 — Lower D2 density. You might get a bigger dopamine rush from watching exciting plays, potentially making sports fandom extra rewarding.

description: Watching sports activates reward and mirror neuron systems in the brain. D2 receptor density can influence how much vicarious thrill you get from spectating. But whether you watch sports is mostly about culture, social bonds, and what teams your family followed.
source: Comings & Blum, Progress in Brain Research 2000

genotype_results.CC: Higher D2 receptor expression in the striatum — standard reward from spectator activities.
genotype_results.CT: Slightly altered D2 expression — may experience moderately enhanced emotional engagement when watching exciting events.
genotype_results.TT: Lower D2 mRNA stability — reduced receptor expression may heighten the appeal of exciting, emotionally charged spectacles like sports.

description: DRD2 C957T (rs6277) affects D2 receptor expression in the brain's reward circuitry. This can subtly influence how much vicarious excitement you experience watching competitive events. Your sports habits are really about social connections, childhood exposure, and culture.
source: Hirvonen et al., Human Brain Mapping 2009

genotype_results.TT: Standard melatonin receptor function — your chronotype is probably shaped more by habits than this variant.
genotype_results.TC: Slightly altered melatonin signaling — may have a mild shift in natural sleep-wake timing.
genotype_results.CC: Modified MTNR1B function — associated with later melatonin onset and evening preference. Your body's clock may naturally run a bit late.

description: MTNR1B codes for the melatonin 1B receptor, which influences when your body starts producing melatonin in the evening. This variant adds to the picture beyond the classic CLOCK gene for a more complete chronotype profile. Light exposure, screen habits, and schedule matter far more.

genotype_results.AA: Morning chronotype tendency — you're probably at your best before noon. Early bird gets the worm, and you might actually enjoy the early alarm.
genotype_results.AG: Intermediate chronotype — you can adapt to most schedules without too much pain.
genotype_results.GG: Evening chronotype — 'night owl' tendencies. Your brain perks up when others are winding down. Peak creativity might hit after dinner.

description: The classic CLOCK 3111T/C variant, revisited here as part of an enhanced chronotype panel. Combined with MTNR1B results, this gives a fuller picture of your natural sleep-wake preferences. But remember — consistent sleep schedules and light exposure can override genetics.
